title: Map device profile to structure
summary: Add two new VisualParams fields — curveSoftness (driven by touch capability) and structureComplexity (driven by DPR, cores, and screen aspect ratio) — and wire them through evolution, rendering, and tests so that device/browser hints visibly influence scene structure while keeping raw values hidden.

steps.action: Extend VisualParams with two new structural fields
steps.details: Add `curveSoftness: number` (0–1, how rounded/organic shapes are) and `structureComplexity: number` (0–1, how intricate the structural layout is) to the VisualParams interface. curveSoftness 0 = angular/sharp, 1 = round/diffuse. structureComplexity 0 = minimal, 1 = rich. Add JSDoc comments matching the existing style.

steps.action: Add two internal mapping helper functions
steps.details: Create `touchToSoftness(touchCapable: boolean | null): number` — returns 0.8 when touch-capable (rounder, finger-friendly shapes), 0.3 when not (crisper, pointer-oriented shapes), and 0.5 as default when null. Create `profileToComplexity(dpr: number | null, cores: number | null, screenWidth: number, screenHeight: number): number` — combines DPR score (weight 0.3), core score (weight 0.4), and aspect-ratio modulation (weight 0.3). Aspect ratio: compute `Math.min(w, h) / Math.max(w, h)` (guard against division by zero when both are 0, defaulting ratio to 1.0). Wider/ultrawide screens (lower ratio) get slightly higher complexity; square-ish screens get slightly lower. Map the weighted sum to 0.2–1.0 range. Note: screenWidth and screenHeight are always numbers (never null) from readSignals() but may be 0 if detection fails — guard the division accordingly.

In the return object of `mapSignalsToVisuals()` (line ~201), add: `curveSoftness: touchToSoftness(signals.touchCapable)` and `structureComplexity: profileToComplexity(signals.devicePixelRatio, signals.hardwareConcurrency, signals.screenWidth, signals.screenHeight)`. This keeps the function pure — no new inputs needed since BrowserSignals already contains all required fields.

steps.action: Update evolveParams to pass through new fields
steps.details: In the return object of `evolveParams()` (after line 116), add pass-through lines: `curveSoftness: base.curveSoftness,` and `structureComplexity: base.structureComplexity,`. These are device-derived structural parameters that should remain stable within a session (no drift), matching the pattern used for density and motionAmplitude. This prevents a TypeScript compile error since the returned object literal must satisfy the full VisualParams interface.

steps.action: Use curveSoftness in particle rendering to modulate shape
steps.details: In the draw function, replace the `ctx.fillRect(...)` block (lines 95–100) with shape logic driven by `params.curveSoftness`. When curveSoftness >= 0.5, render circular particles using `ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(cx, cy, radius, 0, Math.PI * 2); ctx.fill();` where radius = size / 2. When curveSoftness < 0.5, keep the existing `ctx.fillRect(...)`. This creates a visible structural difference: touch devices see rounded, organic particles; desktop pointer devices see angular, sharp particles. The threshold approach avoids per-frame branching overhead on a hot loop — the curveSoftness value is stable within a session so the branch prediction will be consistent.

steps.action: Use structureComplexity to modulate particle count and size variance
steps.details: In the `init()` function, use `params.structureComplexity` alongside density to scale the effective particle count: `effectiveCount = Math.floor(count * (0.6 + params.structureComplexity * 0.4))` — this means low-complexity devices get 60% of density-driven count, high-complexity get 100%. Also scale the size variance during init: higher structureComplexity = wider size distribution (more visual hierarchy), lower = more uniform sizes. In the draw function, scale the hue offset spread: `hueOffset * (0.5 + params.structureComplexity * 0.5)` so complex scenes have richer color variety.

The `computeDefaultParams()` function (line 42) calls `mapSignalsToVisuals()` with defaultSignals. Since mapSignalsToVisuals now returns curveSoftness and structureComplexity, and defaultSignals has touchCapable=null, screenWidth=0, screenHeight=0, the defaults will naturally produce curveSoftness=0.5 and structureComplexity at a reasonable mid-range. Verify this works correctly — no code change needed here unless the defaults produce unexpected values. If screenWidth/Height=0 triggers the division guard in profileToComplexity, it should default gracefully.

steps.action: Update evolution tests for new pass-through fields
steps.details: Add curveSoftness and structureComplexity to the base VisualParams objects used in existing tests. Add a test case verifying that evolveParams passes through curveSoftness and structureComplexity unchanged (same pattern as density/motionAmplitude tests). Ensure that the values remain exactly equal to base values after evolution at multiple elapsed times.

steps.action: Add mapping tests for new structural parameters
steps.details: Add new test cases to the existing file: (1) touchToSoftness returns 0.8 for touch-capable devices, 0.3 for non-touch, 0.5 for null; (2) structureComplexity is in 0.2–1.0 range for various DPR/core/screen combos; (3) structureComplexity handles screenWidth=0 and screenHeight=0 without NaN or Infinity; (4) verify no raw browser strings appear in curveSoftness or structureComplexity output; (5) mapping is stable — calling mapSignalsToVisuals twice with same inputs produces identical curveSoftness and structureComplexity. Update any existing tests that construct VisualParams to include the two new fields.

steps.action: Add particle rendering tests for curveSoftness and structureComplexity
steps.details: Add tests verifying: (1) when curveSoftness >= 0.5, the draw function calls ctx.arc (not fillRect) — spy on ctx.arc and ctx.fillRect; (2) when curveSoftness < 0.5, the draw function calls ctx.fillRect (not arc); (3) structureComplexity affects effective particle count (lower complexity = fewer particles for same density); (4) scene still renders without errors when curveSoftness and structureComplexity are at boundary values (0 and 1). Update existing test VisualParams fixtures to include the new fields.

steps.action: Build verification and manual smoke test
steps.details: Run `npm run build` to verify no TypeScript errors. Run `npm run test` to verify all tests pass. Manually verify in browser: (1) scene renders without console errors; (2) no raw browser strings visible anywhere; (3) toggling touch emulation in DevTools and reloading shows different particle shapes; (4) changing DPR in DevTools and reloading shows different structural complexity; (5) mute toggle and info overlay still work; (6) scene works when signals are partially unavailable.

acceptance_verification.at_least_two_structural_parameters_drive: curveSoftness is driven by touchCapable signal; structureComplexity is driven by DPR, hardwareConcurrency, and screen dimensions — totaling two distinct structural parameters from four signal sources.
acceptance_verification.no_raw_browser_strings_shown: All mappings produce normalized numbers (0–1 range). No user-agent, screen resolution text, or touch capability booleans appear in visual output. Verified by tests.
acceptance_verification.mapping_stable_within_session: Both new params are derived deterministically from BrowserSignals which don't change during a session. evolveParams passes them through unchanged (no drift). Verified by evolution and mapping stability tests.
acceptance_verification.scene_works_if_signals_missing: touchToSoftness defaults to 0.5 when null. profileToComplexity has null guards on DPR/cores and zero-division guard on screen dimensions. computeDefaultParams produces valid fallback values. Verified by null-handling tests.
